my_sentence: Not beef soup. Just a beef.
correction: Not beef soup. Just beef.
explanation: "Beef" is an uncountable noun, so it doesn't take the indefinite article "a" — say "beef," not "a beef."
correct_examples: I only had beef for dinner, not beef soup.
She eats chicken, not a chicken, for lunch.
context: Describing what he ate for dinner.

my_sentence: If you have a garden, you need to spend a lot of time to planter or fix something in it, like planting or watering and taking care of sunflowers.
correction: If you have a garden, you need to spend a lot of time planting, watering, and taking care of flowers.
explanation: "Spend time" is followed by a gerund (spend time doing something), not "to + verb"; also "planter" isn't an English verb — use "planting."
correct_examples: I spend a lot of time cleaning the house.
She spends hours watering her plants every week.
context: Talking about why he doesn't want a garden.

my_sentence: If they make a mistake, their patients will lose their lives. The patient can lose life.
correction: If they make a mistake, a patient can lose his or her life.
explanation: Use a possessive pronoun before "life" ("his or her life"); also refer to a general case with singular "a patient," not "their patients."
correct_examples: If the driver is careless, a passenger could lose his or her life.
One mistake, and a patient can lose his or her life.
context: Discussing the cons of being a doctor.

my_sentence: Do doctors need to be carefully?
correction: Doctors need to be careful.
explanation: After the linking verb "be," use the adjective "careful," not the adverb "carefully."
correct_examples: Please be careful when you cross the street.
You need to be careful with sharp knives.
context: Discussing doctors needing to be careful during operations.

my_sentence: There are too many patients who stayed in the hospital to look for an operation.
correction: There are too many patients who stayed in the hospital waiting for an operation.
explanation: "Look for" means to search for something; the intended meaning here is "waiting for," not searching.
correct_examples: Many patients are waiting for surgery.
He's been waiting for a bed in the hospital for days.
context: Discussing why doctors are overworked in big-city hospitals.

my_sentence: You can order a doctor in any time... I need to order a doctor in the city's hospital.
correction: You can make an appointment with a doctor at any time... I need to book an appointment with a doctor at the city's hospital.
explanation: (assistant note) The natural collocation for arranging a doctor's visit is "make/book an appointment," not "order a doctor."
correct_examples: I need to book an appointment with a specialist.
She made an appointment with her doctor for next week.
context: Talking about seeing doctors in different cities.

my_sentence: You don't need to wait a long time.
correction: You don't need to wait for a long time.
explanation: Use the preposition "for" before a duration with "wait": "wait for a long time."
correct_examples: We had to wait for a long time at the airport.
You won't have to wait for long.
context: Comparing the wait time to see a doctor in different cities.

my_sentence: They only live in big cities. It's a rarely can be found in small cities.
correction: It's rare that they cannot be found in small cities.
explanation: "Rare" is an adjective, not "a rarely" ("rarely" is an adverb and doesn't take the article "a"); build the sentence as "it's rare that + clause."
correct_examples: It's rare that you see snow here.
It's rare for experts to work in small towns.
context: Discussing the shortage of medical experts in small cities.

my_sentence: They want to live in big cities, not small cities.
correction: They want to live in big cities, not in the small one.
explanation: Avoid repeating the noun "cities" — replace it with a pronoun ("the small one"), as the teacher modeled, instead of repeating the noun.
correct_examples: I prefer the red one, not the blue one.
She lives in the countryside, not in the city.
context: Discussing why experts prefer big cities.

my_sentence: We can't find them in other places aside from the big one.
correction: We can't find them in other places aside from the big ones.
explanation: "Cities" is plural, so refer back to it with the plural "ones," not the singular "one."
correct_examples: Most stores are closed except the big ones.
I like all the photos except the small ones.
context: Continuing the discussion about where experts live.

my_sentence: Its change, it became, it became capital.
correction: It changed. It became the capital city.
explanation: Use the past-tense verb "changed," not the noun "change."
correct_examples: The city changed a lot over the years.
Beijing changed from a small town into a huge capital.
context: Discussing when Beijing became China's capital.

my_sentence: I think that's because the King want to protect the north part of China.
correction: I think that's because the king wanted to protect the northern part of China.
explanation: Use the past tense "wanted" to match the historical narrative, not the present "want."
correct_examples: The emperor wanted to defend the northern border.
They wanted to protect their territory.
context: Explaining why the capital moved to Beijing.

my_sentence: Wanted to protect the north part of China... it's the center of the south part of China.
correction: Wanted to protect the northern part of China... it's the center of the southern part of China.
explanation: Use the adjective forms "northern"/"southern" as modifiers before "part," not the bare directions "north"/"south."
correct_examples: He grew up in the southern part of the country.
The war started in the northern part of the region.
context: Discussing the geography of China's dynasties.

my_sentence: A astronaut can let people travel to space and see different planets.
correction: An astronaut can let people travel to space and see different planets.
explanation: Use "an" before a word starting with a vowel sound ("astronaut"), not "a."
correct_examples: An engineer designs new machines.
She wants to become an astronaut one day.
context: Talking about the pros of being an astronaut.

my_sentence: Being an astronaut can travel to space.
correction: Being an astronaut can let a person travel to space and see different planets.
explanation: "Being an astronaut" itself cannot "travel" — use a causative structure ("can let a person...") so the gerund phrase works correctly as the subject.
correct_examples: Being a doctor can let you help many people.
Being a pilot can let someone see the world.
context: Talking about the pros of being an astronaut.

my_sentence: Understand the word. (intending "understand the world")
correction: Understand the world.
explanation: "Word" and "world" differ by the "l" sound — the tip of the tongue must touch just behind the upper teeth to produce the "l" in "world," or it will sound like "word."
correct_examples: I want to see the world.
This changed my view of the world.
context: Practicing the pronunciation of "world" vs. "word."

my_sentence: They do some scientific research to help us understand the universal.
correction: They do some scientific research to help us understand the universe.
explanation: "Universe" is the noun (all of space); "universal" is an adjective meaning "applying to everything" — don't confuse the two.
correct_examples: Scientists study the universe.
This rule is a universal truth.
context: Discussing the pros of being an astronaut.

my_sentence: They do some scientific research out of space.
correction: They do some scientific research in outer space.
explanation: The correct phrase is "in outer space"; "out of space" means something else entirely ("no more room").
correct_examples: Astronauts conduct experiments in outer space.
The satellite orbits in outer space.
context: Discussing what astronauts do in space.

my_sentence: I mean for some people, he or she spent many years to form a habit.
correction: I mean, if they form a habit for many years, they can't change their ideas and behaviors easily.
explanation: Keep the generic subject pronoun consistent — use "they" throughout instead of switching to "he or she" mid-sentence.
correct_examples: If a person practices for years, they get very skilled.
Once someone forms a habit, they rarely break it easily.
context: Discussing whether criminals can change after seeing Earth from space.

my_sentence: I was very curious, so to explore something. It is a mystery.
correction: I was very curious about mysterious things.
explanation: Use the adjective form "mysterious things" as the object of "curious about," rather than saying "it is a mystery."
correct_examples: Children are often curious about mysterious things.
I've always been curious about ancient history.
context: Talking about his childhood dream of becoming an astronaut.

my_sentence: I needed to learn many thing.
correction: I needed to learn many things.
explanation: "Thing" needs the plural form "things" after "many."
correct_examples: We learned many things in school.
There are many things to consider.
context: Talking about growing up and forgetting his childhood dream.

my_sentence: I needed to learn many things at school and gradually I forgot my dream.
correction: I needed to learn many things at school and gradually I forgot all about it.
explanation: The more natural idiom here is "forgot all about it," rather than restating "forgot my dream."
correct_examples: He forgot all about his promise.
I forgot all about the meeting.
context: Talking about forgetting his childhood dream of becoming an astronaut.

my_sentence: Everyone can take a plan to go to travel out space.
correction: Everyone can plan to go to outer space.
explanation: "Plan" is used as a verb here ("plan to do something"), not as a noun with "take a plan"; also the phrase is "outer space," not "out space."
correct_examples: We plan to travel to Japan next year.
She plans to study abroad.
context: Speculating about ordinary people traveling to space in the future.

my_sentence: They need to train a lot for there.
correction: If someone wants to be an astronaut, he or she needs to train a lot for it.
explanation: Build the sentence with a clear conditional clause ("if someone wants to be an astronaut, ...") rather than a vague pronoun reference.
correct_examples: If someone wants to become a pilot, they need years of training.
If you want to be a doctor, you need to study hard.
context: Discussing the training required to become an astronaut.

my_sentence: You can easily to get.
correction: You can easily get it.
explanation: After the modal verb "can," use the bare infinitive (no "to"): "can easily get," not "can easily to get."
correct_examples: You can easily find it online.
She can easily solve this problem.
context: Discussing the pros of being a computer engineer (open-source resources).

my_sentence: You can easily get learn and communicate with them.
correction: You can easily learn and communicate with the authors.
explanation: Don't stack two verbs ("get learn"); just use the single verb "learn."
correct_examples: You can easily learn a new skill online.
He can easily learn new languages.
context: Discussing open-source resources in computer engineering.

my_sentence: If you produce a water, you only can sell it to one person.
correction: If you produce water, you can only sell it to one person.
explanation: "Water" is uncountable, so it doesn't take the indefinite article "a."
correct_examples: Factories produce water for the whole city.
This plant produces clean water.
context: Comparing physical products to software.

my_sentence: If you produce water, you only can sell it to one person.
correction: If you produce water, you can only sell it to one person.
explanation: The adverb "only" goes after the modal "can," not before it: "can only sell," not "only can sell."
correct_examples: You can only rent an apartment downtown.
He can only work part-time.
context: Comparing physical products to software.

my_sentence: You can sell it to hundreds of millions of people. (referring to software services)
correction: You can give services to hundreds of millions of people.
explanation: For software/services, the natural collocation is "give/provide services," not "sell," when emphasizing reach.
correct_examples: The app provides services to millions of users.
This platform gives services to people worldwide.
context: Discussing how software scales compared to physical products.

my_sentence: One person use one person usage, Tencent.
correction: One person's usage of Tencent Meeting.
explanation: Use the possessive apostrophe-s ("a person's usage") to show whose usage it is, not the bare noun.
correct_examples: This is my sister's car.
The company's profit doubled this year.
context: Discussing the cost of a person using Tencent Meeting.

my_sentence: If a person uses the Tencent Meeting, the cost is the same four hundreds of people.
correction: If a person uses Tencent Meeting, the cost is the same for hundreds of people.
explanation: "For" (preposition) and "four" (number) are homophones — the intended word here is "for," not "four."
correct_examples: This price is the same for everyone.
The plan works for hundreds of users.
context: Explaining that software scales at the same cost.

my_sentence: The cost for a person and 100 people to use Tencent Meeting is the same.
correction: The cost for a person and hundreds of people who use Tencent Meeting is the same.
explanation: Use the relative pronoun "who" to introduce the clause describing the people, rather than an infinitive "to use."
correct_examples: The teacher who taught us math retired.
People who use this app love it.
context: Explaining the cost structure of software services.

my_sentence: The Tencent Company deploys their services like the Tencent Meeting, who needs to...
correction: The Tencent Company deploys their services like the Tencent Meeting, that needs to...
explanation: Use "that/which" for things (like an application), not "who," which is reserved for people.
correct_examples: The app, which needs an update, crashed yesterday.
This is the software that needs fixing.
context: Explaining how companies deploy software services.

my_sentence: If you want to deploy your services, you need to prepare some virtual machines.
correction: If you want to give your services, you need to deploy your application on some electronic devices.
explanation: "Deploy" collocates with "application" (the technical setup), while "give/provide" collocates with "services" — don't swap them.
correct_examples: We deployed the new application last night.
The company provides services to thousands of customers.
context: Explaining how to run an online service.

my_sentence: Virtual machines like computers or laptops.
correction: Electronic devices like computers or laptops.
explanation: "Virtual machine" is a specific technical term (a software-based computer); for general physical devices, use "electronic devices."
correct_examples: You need electronic devices like a phone or laptop.
The store sells many electronic devices.
context: Explaining what's needed to run an application.

my_sentence: This idea to. Or so naive. (intending "their ideas are so naive")
correction: Their ideas are so naive.
explanation: Use the plural possessive "their ideas," matching the plural referent, not the singular "this idea."
correct_examples: Their plans are too naive.
His ideas are still very simple.
context: Discussing the cons of being a computer engineer (lack of experience).

my_sentence: They don't know many in the world.
correction: They don't know much about the world.
explanation: (assistant note) "Many" is for countable nouns; the intended meaning ("not much knowledge") needs "much" and the phrase "about the world."
correct_examples: He doesn't know much about cooking.
I don't know much about finance.
context: Discussing why new engineers seem naive.

my_sentence: If some of them meet any problem, they don't know how to fix it.
correction: If any of them meets some problems, they don't know how to fix them.
explanation: With "any of them" as the subject, the verb should agree in the third-person singular ("meets"), and plural "problems" pairs naturally with "some."
correct_examples: If any of them needs help, let me know.
If any of the students has questions, raise your hand.
context: Discussing coworkers who struggle to communicate with strangers.

my_sentence: If they don't try to strive for their interests, no one will consider for them.
correction: If they don't try to strive for their interests, no one will consider them.
explanation: "Consider" takes a direct object without a preposition ("consider them"), not "consider for them."
correct_examples: The manager will consider your request.
No one considered my opinion.
context: Discussing self-interest in the workplace.

my_sentence: We learned many things about sharing and considering others during our childhood, but reality, one way, when we face some interest, we just consider for our own.
correction: We learned many things about sharing and considering others during our childhood, but in reality, when we follow our own interests, we want to get more.
explanation: The fixed phrase is "in reality" (with the preposition "in"), not "reality" alone.
correct_examples: In theory it sounds simple, but in reality it's hard.
In reality, things rarely go as planned.
context: Discussing self-interest versus childhood values.

my_sentence: When we share some interests together, we want to get more.
correction: When we follow our own interests together, we want to get more.
explanation: The intended meaning here is "follow one's own interests" (pursue what benefits you), not "share interests."
correct_examples: Everyone follows their own interests at work.
He always follows his own interests first.
context: Discussing self-interest in group work.

my_sentence: Someone can get more and others can get less.
correction: Someone can get more and others have less.
explanation: Avoid repeating the same verb ("get") in parallel clauses — vary it ("get more... have less") for natural style.
correct_examples: Some win big and others lose a little.
Some earn a lot and others make very little.
context: Discussing unequal sharing of profits in a team.

my_sentence: I think the society is so complexity.
correction: I think society is so complex.
explanation: After "is," use the adjective "complex," not the noun "complexity."
correct_examples: Modern life is very complex.
This problem is more complex than it looks.
context: Discussing competition and cooperation in society.

my_sentence: Whenever I go out, I was customer.
correction: Whenever I go out, I will be a customer.
explanation: Use the future tense "will be" for this general/habitual scenario, not the past "was," and include the article "a" before the singular noun "customer."
correct_examples: Whenever I travel, I will be a tourist there.
When I visit that store, I will be a customer.
context: Talking about buying products and services.

my_sentence: I spend money to buy services.
correction: I spend my own money to get some services.
explanation: The natural collocation here is "get services" (or "get a service"), not "buy services."
correct_examples: I paid to get a haircut.
We get cleaning services once a week.
context: Talking about spending money as a customer.

my_sentence: So I smile a lot often.
correction: So I smile a lot more often.
explanation: "A lot" can intensify a comparative ("a lot more often"), but it can't just sit next to "often" without the comparative "more" connecting them.
correct_examples: I exercise more often now.
She visits her parents more often since moving closer.
context: Talking about smiling more when spending time with family.

my_sentence: I am not familiar about everyone around my neighborhood.
correction: I am not familiar with everyone around my neighborhood.
explanation: (assistant note) "Familiar" is followed by the preposition "with," not "about."
correct_examples: I'm not familiar with this software.
She is familiar with the local customs.
context: Talking about not knowing his neighbors well.

my_sentence: As a patient or as ordinary people, you don't know anything about medicine.
correction: As a patient or as an ordinary person, you don't know anything about medicine.
explanation: Keep the parallel structure singular ("a patient... an ordinary person"), matching number on both sides of "or."
correct_examples: As a student or as a teacher, you have responsibilities.
As a parent or as an employee, time management matters.
context: Discussing patients' rights to ask doctors questions.

my_sentence: You don't know anything about medicine, anything about medicine or processes.
correction: You don't know many things about medicine or its processes.
explanation: Avoid repeating "anything about medicine," and use the possessive "its processes" to refer back to "medicine."
correct_examples: I don't know much about this company or its history.
She doesn't understand chemistry or its formulas.
context: Discussing patients' lack of medical knowledge.

my_sentence: If she change a new one, it's...
correction: If she changed to a new company, ...
explanation: "Change" needs the preposition "to" before the destination ("change to a new company"), and the past tense "changed" fits this hypothetical scenario.
correct_examples: He changed to a different job last year.
She changed to a new school in September.
context: Discussing an employee who couldn't get promoted.

my_sentence: I don't think it's a short years. Three to five years are so long.
correction: I don't think it's a short time. Three to five years is a long time.
explanation: (assistant note) Don't use the singular article "a" with the plural noun "years" — say "a short time," or drop the article and keep "years" plural without "a."
correct_examples: Two years is a long time to wait.
That's not a short time to spend on one project.
context: Discussing how long a delay in promotion feels.

my_sentence: You don't get promotion.
correction: You don't get promoted.
explanation: After "get" (meaning "become"), use the past participle "promoted," not the noun "promotion."
correct_examples: She got promoted last month.
If you work hard, you'll get promoted.
context: Explaining what happens if you stay at the same level for years.

my_sentence: You will feel terrible and unfair.
correction: You will feel terrible about it and think it's unfair.
explanation: "Unfair" describes a judgment, not a feeling — say "think it's unfair" rather than "feel unfair."
correct_examples: He felt bad about the decision and thought it was unjust.
I feel upset about it and think it's not right.
context: Discussing how it feels to be passed over for a promotion.

my_sentence: Today I did some tasks in my work.
correction: Today I did some tasks at work.
explanation: Use the fixed phrase "at work" for being at one's job, not "in my work."
correct_examples: I was at work all day.
She stayed late at work yesterday.
context: Describing his day when asked what he did.

my_sentence: I think most of the authors willing to share their ideas and method to you for free.
correction: I think most of the authors are willing to share their ideas and methods with you for free.
explanation: The adjective "willing" needs the auxiliary "be" ("are willing") to form a complete verb group — it can't follow the subject directly.
correct_examples: Most teachers are willing to help after class.
The company is willing to negotiate the price.
context: Discussing the open-source culture in software engineering.
